directions.name: Restore the accepted-correct hot-band surface and apply a warp-local B XOR/interleaved consumer swizzle with zero extra shared footprint
directions.hypothesis: Round 12 showed that spending extra shared-memory budget on a deeper pipeline did not buy real hot-band speed: the main 256x128 kernel moved only from about 41.11 ms to 41.18 ms while barrier stalls jumped from about 6.59% to 20.24%, and correctness broke on top. That weakens the stage family for now. The user's remaining strongest untried constraint set is consumer-side B feed repair with three hard gates: no extra shared tile, no extra CTA barrier, no reduction in the steady-state pipeline. The current hot-band kernel still uses only a simple warp-group skew on B shared storage. A light XOR or interleaved remap in the logical B shared column mapping is the best next shot because it targets operand delivery directly while honoring those gates.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory bank behavior and warp-local B operand delivery in the hot-band PTX consumer path.
directions.risk: High. A broken remap silently corrupts results, and even a correct remap can worsen global-to-shared coalescing or register use enough to erase any bank benefit.
directions.stop_condition: Reject immediately if correctness fails, shared memory per block increases, or hot-band time does not beat the restored accepted surface.

directions.name: Restore the accepted surface and start an explicit ldmatrix/mma.sync hot-band microkernel branch
directions.hypothesis: The user has already pointed out that the accepted base still sits at only about 38.6% tensor active, and the recent diagnosis rounds keep circling feed/orchestration rather than raw DRAM saturation. At this point the repo has explored several scheduling variants inside the WMMA-based PTX control plane, but not the higher-ceiling move of taking fragment residency, lane mapping, and operand load order fully into an explicit ldmatrix/mma.sync microkernel for the 256x128 hot band only. That branch is more expensive to build, but it is the clearest path left to materially change how the dominant kernel feeds Tensor Cores.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Tensor Core under-utilization caused by the current WMMA-based fragment load and issue model rather than tile shape alone.
directions.risk: Very high. This is effectively a new hot-band microkernel with a larger correctness surface and a longer implementation/debug cycle.
directions.stop_condition: Defer or reject if the lighter bank-feed experiment wins, or if the explicit microkernel cannot be landed as a single hot-band-only branch.

directions.name: Restore the accepted-correct implementation surface before the next experiment
directions.hypothesis: The current source is on an incorrect 3-stage branch. Before any new exploration, the repo needs to recover a known-good hot-band surface so that subsequent results are comparable against the accepted baseline instead of against a failing experiment.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Not a bottleneck attack; this is the reset path that keeps later rounds interpretable.
directions.stop_condition: Use this if the higher-ranked branches do not build cleanly or fail correctness.

notes: Round 13/50 shifts away from the stage family because the 3-stage hot-band branch only moved the dominant kernel from about 41.11 ms to 41.18 ms while breaking correctness and driving barrier stalls much higher. The recommended next family is the user's consumer-side B feed idea under strict constraints: no extra shared footprint, no extra CTA barriers, and no CTA-level repack.
